plainMeaning: Obey the Jinas' command, give up wrong belief, hold firmly to right faith, and be diligent every single day in the six essential duties.
recitation: mannaha jiṇāṇaṁ āṇaṁ, micchaṁ pariharaha dharaha sammattaṁ | chavviha āvassayammi, ujjutto hoi païdivasaṁ ||

wordByWord.etymology: From Skt. root man- ('to think, regard, hold in mind'); Prakrit 2nd-person plural imperative manyadhvam → mannaha.
wordByWord.notes: The verb governs āṇaṁ in the next words: 'accept the command'. Plural imperative addresses the whole assembly of śrāvakas.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. jinānām, genitive plural of jina ('victor, conqueror of passions'); Prakrit -āṇaṁ genitive plural ending.
wordByWord.notes: Read in close sandhi with the following āṇaṁ ('the Jinas' command'); printed editions often write the joined form jiṇāṇamāṇaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ājñā → Prakrit āṇā; accusative singular āṇaṁ. The object of mannaha.
wordByWord.notes: Already glossed in the preface as the Jina's command; here it is the grammatical object 'heed [the] command'.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. mithyātva / mithyā → Prakrit micchā; accusative micchaṁ. Object of pariharaha.
wordByWord.notes: Preface term mithyātva; here the thing to be discarded.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. pari-√hṛ ('to remove, give up entirely'), imperative plural pariharata → Prakrit pariharaha.
wordByWord.notes: The pari- prefix intensifies: not mere avoidance but total renunciation of false belief.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. √dhṛ ('to hold, bear, sustain'), imperative plural dharata → Prakrit dharaha.
wordByWord.notes: Deliberately paired against pariharaha: cast off X, hold Y — the negative-then-positive movement of the line.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. samyaktva → Prakrit sammatta; accusative sammattaṁ. Object of dharaha.
wordByWord.notes: Preface term samyaktva (samyag-darśana); the thing to be held.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ṣaḍvidha ('six-fold'); ṣaṭ + vidha. Prakrit doubles the consonant: chavviha.
wordByWord.notes: Qualifies āvassaya; together 'the six-fold Āvaśyaka'. Not 'four-fold' (a common loose mistranslation).

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. āvaśyaka ('that which must be done') + Prakrit locative -mmi (Skt. -smin). āvassaya + (a)mmi.
wordByWord.notes: Preface term ṣaḍ-āvaśyaka; locative marks the sphere in which one is to be diligent.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. udyukta ('exerted, applied, zealous'), past participle of ud-√yuj ('to yoke up, set to work'); Prakrit ujjutto.
wordByWord.notes: Predicate adjective with hoi: 'let [one] be diligent'. Connotes active, harnessed effort, not passive presence.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bhavati (root √bhū, 'to be, become') → Prakrit hoi.
wordByWord.notes: Third-person form read with imperative/exhortative force here, completing the predicate ujjutto hoi ('one is to be diligent').

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. prati-divasam ('each day'); prati ('every') + divasa ('day'), adverbial accusative. Prakrit softens prati → paï.
wordByWord.notes: Adverb of frequency governing ujjutto hoi; stresses unbroken daily regularity, not occasional practice.

literalTranslation: Heed the command of the Jinas; abandon false belief, hold right faith. In the six-fold obligatory observance let one be diligent every day.
idiomaticTranslation: Honor the teaching of the Victors as your authority; cast off all wrong belief and hold fast to right faith — and in the six daily duties be earnestly engaged, day after day without fail.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This opening verse lays the doctrinal footing on which every later duty in the hymn rests, and it does so through four crisp imperatives. First comes acceptance of authority: mannaha jiṇāṇaṁ āṇaṁ — 'heed the Jinas' command.' Before any practice is named, the reciter is told whose word grounds it; Jain conduct is āṇā-pradhāna, 'command-governed,' so right action begins by submitting one's judgment to the Jina's teaching. The next two clauses move as a deliberate pair — pariharaha / dharaha, 'cast off' and 'hold fast' — turning the soul first away from micchaṁ (false belief) and then toward sammattaṁ (right faith). The order is exact and not interchangeable: delusion must be released before correct insight can be firmly grasped. Placing this purification of belief before the list of observances signals that samyaktva is the root; works performed without right faith do not yet count as the śrāvaka's dharma. Only then does the verse name action: chavviha āvassayammi ujjutto hoi païdivasaṁ — 'be diligent in the six obligatory observances, every day.' Two words carry the weight here. Ujjutto (Skt. udyukta) is not mere attendance but harnessed, zealous effort — the self 'yoked up' to the task. And païdivasaṁ insists on daily, unbroken regularity: the six Āvaśyakas are not festival acts but the texture of ordinary days. Thus the verse compresses the whole logic of lay discipline — accept the teaching, correct the belief, then act, and act constantly — and so prepares the widening catalogue of duties that the following verse begins to unfold with the poṣadha vow and the four-fold dharma.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The line enacts, in miniature, the Jain ratnatraya sequence: right faith (samyag-darśana) is named first and made foundational, exactly as Umāsvāti opens the path with samyag-darśana before jñāna and cāritra. Mithyātva, its opposite, is treated as the primary bondage to be severed — its abandonment is the threshold of the guṇasthāna ladder. The structuring of the whole lay life around the ṣaḍ-āvaśyaka reflects the Āvaśyaka tradition's insistence that these six are 'that which must be done' (āvaśyaka = obligatory) daily, irrespective of mood or circumstance. And the framing imperative mannaha…āṇaṁ encodes the doctrine that dharma is rooted in the Jina's command (āṇāe dhammo): authority precedes, and validates, observance.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited at the start of the duty-list, this verse asks the practitioner to audit two things each day: is my belief still aligned with the Jina's teaching (have I quietly let in fear-, greed-, or status-driven notions?), and have I actually performed the six daily observances, or merely intended them? Païdivasaṁ is the standard — consistency over intensity. It reframes routine practice not as ritual habit but as a daily renewal of faith expressed in action.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 1.1: samyag-darśana-jñāna-cāritrāṇi mokṣa-mārgaḥ (right faith named first of the three jewels)
Āvaśyaka-niryukti — exposition of the six obligatory observances (ṣaḍ-āvaśyaka) and the duty of daily performance

notes: Token coverage is 100% against the verse as supplied. Variant reading: the prompt prints jiṇāṇaṁ and āṇaṁ as separate tokens, whereas common printed editions (e.g. jainknowledge.com) show the sandhi-joined jiṇāṇamāṇaṁ; the sense ('the Jinas' command') is identical and the prompt's verbatim split is followed here. chavviha (ṣaḍvidha, 'six-fold') is occasionally mis-glossed online as 'four-fold' and païdivasaṁ as 'merit' — both are incorrect; the verse means 'diligent in the six [Āvaśyakas], daily.' hoi (Skt. bhavati) is third-person in form but is read here with imperative/exhortative force ('let one be'), matching the surrounding plural imperatives mannaha/pariharaha/dharaha. Per the preface, glossary terms (āṇā, mithyātva, samyaktva, ṣaḍ-āvaśyaka) are used without re-definition.
